commonProblems.h2: Common Peugeot Engine Problems — What It Costs to Fix & When Replacement Makes Sense
commonProblems.h3: Peugeot’s UK lineup—particularly the 208, 2008, 3008, Partner, Expert, and Boxer—has faced significant reliability challenges around wet-belt petrol engines (1.2 PureTech) and diesel injection systems (1.6 BlueHDi). Private owners search for "cheap used engines" after catastrophic failure, while van operators prioritise "turnaround time" and "supply and fit" warranties. Every rebuilt Peugeot engine supplied through EngineMarket includes upgraded internal components specifically designed to address the original failure point—plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. Below, we break down the most common failures, what they cost to fix (dealer vs. specialist), and when replacement is the only sensible answer.

problems.h4: 1.2 PureTech Wet Belt Degradation – The Timing Belt That Dissolves
problems.affectedModels: Peugeot 208, 2008, 308, 3008, Rifter (2014–present, EB2, EB2ADTX, EB2DTS, HM01, HNZ engines)
problems.rootCause: Unlike a conventional dry timing belt, the 1.2 PureTech’s belt runs inside the engine oil. Over time, heat and contamination cause the belt’s rubber to degrade, shedding material that blocks the oil pickup strainer, leading to oil starvation.
problems.recommendation: On a Peugeot 208 or 2008 worth £3,000–£6,000, a preventative belt replacement at a specialist (£700–£1,000) is excellent value if you have no warning lights or rattles. However, once the oil pressure warning appears, internal damage is almost certain. In that case, a rebuilt engine with upgraded belt components is the only long‑term solution – dealer prices for repair often exceed the car’s value.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt EB2 / EB2ADTX engine prices for your 208 or 2008

problems.h4: 1.6 BlueHDi AdBlue System Failure – Crystallisation & ECU Lockout
problems.affectedModels: Peugeot 308, 3008, 5008, Expert, Partner (2015–2020, DV6, DV6TED4, 9H06 engines)
problems.rootCause: The AdBlue injector cools after engine shutdown, allowing urea solution to crystallise inside the injector and delivery lines. Once blocked, the NOx efficiency drops, and the ECU triggers a "NO START in X miles" countdown, requiring dealer‑level unlock codes.
problems.recommendation: If your van or car is used for commercial purposes and the NOx countdown has started, the specialist replacement of injector + lines + ECU unlock (£750–£1,100) is the only road‑legal, cost‑effective route. Dealer pricing is typically 60% higher. For long‑term private ownership, a rebuilt engine with upgraded injector seals is worth considering if the vehicle has high mileage.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt DV6 engines with upgraded AdBlue components

problems.h4: 1.6 HDi (DV6) Turbocharger & Oil Supply Failure – The Coking Problem
problems.rootCause: The turbocharger oil feed pipe sits close to exhaust heat, causing oil to coke (carbonise). The restricted oil flow first damages the turbo bearings, then sends metal debris directly into the engine’s bottom end, often wiping out conrod bearings.
problems.recommendation: On a Peugeot 308 or 3008 worth £4,000–£7,000, we do NOT recommend a turbo‑only replacement. The coked oil feed almost always sends debris to the bottom end. A rebuilt DV6 engine with an upgraded, heat‑shielded oil feed line is the only sensible long‑term solution. Specialist rebuilt engines cost significantly less than a dealer’s turbo‑plus‑repair quote.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt DV6 / DV6TED4 engine prices with upgraded turbo lines

problems.h4: 1.5 BlueHDi (DV5) Crankshaft Bearing Failure – The Premature Seizure
problems.affectedModels: Peugeot 3008, 5008, Rifter (2018–2022, DV5RC, DV5R, YHZ, AH01)
problems.rootCause: Manufacturing tolerance issue leads to inadequate oil film thickness on crankshaft main bearings. Under load, bearings wipe, crank drops onto the bedplate, and the engine seizes with no prior warning.
problems.recommendation: On a Peugeot 3008 or 5008 still showing no symptoms, there is no preventative repair – the failure is sudden and catastrophic. If the crankshaft has already seized or run bearings, a rebuilt DV5 engine with corrected bearing clearances is the only safe option. Do not attempt a second‑hand engine from another 2018‑2022 vehicle – it carries the same manufacturing defect.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt DV5 engines with upgraded bearing clearances for your 3008 / 5008

problems.group: 2.0 HDi (DW10) Injector & High-Pressure Pump Seal Failure
problems.h4: 2.0 HDi (DW10) Injector & High‑Pressure Pump Seal Failure – Diesel in the Oil
problems.rootCause: High‑pressure pump shaft seals or piezo injector seals leak diesel fuel directly into the engine oil. The oil level rises, viscosity drops to that of water, and main / big‑end bearings fail rapidly.
problems.recommendation: For commercial Boxer and Expert vans, check your dipstick weekly. If the oil level has risen above max, a specialist minor seal replacement (£450–£700) is excellent value IF the engine hasn’t started knocking. However, once you hear bottom‑end noise, bearing damage is already advanced – a rebuilt DW10 engine with upgraded seals is the only reliable route.
problems.cta: → Compare rebuilt DW10 engine prices for your Boxer or Expert van

problems.group: 1.6 THP (EP6) Timing Chain & High-Pressure Fuel Pump
problems.h4: 1.6 THP (EP6) Timing Chain & High‑Pressure Fuel Pump – The Prince Engine Weaknesses
problems.rootCause: Two distinct but related failures: (1) The timing chain tensioner fails, chain slaps the guides and snaps; (2) The high‑pressure fuel pump (driven by the camshaft) shears its internal drive, dumping metal debris into the camshaft bearings and timing chain.
problems.recommendation: On a Peugeot RCZ or 308, if you hear chain rattle on cold start but no metal in the oil, a specialist timing chain kit (£700–£1,000) is money well spent. However, if the high‑pressure pump has already failed (visible metal in oil filter), do not simply replace the pump – debris has already travelled to cam bearings. A rebuilt EP6 engine is the only sensible outcome.
problems.cta: → Get quotes for rebuilt EP6 / 5G06 engines with uprated timing gear

engineTypes.h2: Peugeot Engine Types – Options, Costs & What to Expect
engineTypes.intro: Every Peugeot engine type available through our UK network, with typical price ranges and recommendations based on your vehicle’s age, condition and your budget.

types.title: Peugeot Reconditioned Engines (Recon)
types.description: A stripped, cleaned and fully inspected engine where all worn components are replaced with OEM‑spec parts. Best for owners who want factory reliability for their PureTech or BlueHDi engine without the main dealer premium.
types.priceRange: Typical price range: £1,800 – £3,500 (depending on engine code)

types.title: Peugeot Rebuilt Engines
types.description: A complete rebuild with upgraded components addressing known failure points – such as revised wet belt systems for EB2 PureTech engines and uprated timing chain kits for EP6 THP units – for a longer service life than a standard replacement.

types.title: Used Peugeot Engines
types.description: A tested, low‑mileage take‑out unit from a donor vehicle. The most affordable option when budget is the priority, with verified history and warranty available – ideal for older HDi models where a full recon isn't economical.

types.title: Peugeot Remanufactured Engines
types.description: A factory‑spec, as‑new engine: every surface is machined, every component is replaced, and the unit is built to meet exact OEM tolerances. The premium choice for RCZ, 208 GTi, or 508 owners wanting a zero‑mile engine.

types.title: Peugeot Refurbished Engines
types.description: A lightly serviced, cleaned, and tested engine that works reliably but hasn't had a complete rebuild. Often a pragmatic, fast solution for older 206, 307 or 406 models where a full recon isn't cost‑effective.

types.title: Peugeot Supply & Fit Engines
types.description: Your replacement engine delivered and professionally installed by a UK Peugeot specialist. Includes removal of the old unit, fitting, fluids, and a workmanship warranty – the complete all‑in‑one solution for van operators needing fast turnaround.
types.priceRange: Typical added cost: £500 – £1,200 on top of engine price
types.cta: Find Peugeot engine supply & fit deals

engineTypes.closing: All engine types include a minimum 12‑month warranty. Prices are typical UK market ranges and depend on exact engine code, supplier, and any additional fitting services. Use our free comparison tool to get a tailored quote for your Peugeot model today.

engineSizes.h2: Peugeot Engines by Size – Find the Right Displacement for Your Model
engineSizes.intro: Every Peugeot engine size explained — with the models and engine codes that used each displacement — so you can quickly identify the correct replacement unit for your vehicle.

items.title: Peugeot 1.4L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.4L diesel was Peugeot’s entry-level oil-burner across the 206, 307, and Partner van ranges, primarily using the DV4 engine family. Known for solid fuel economy, these units are generally robust but suffer from turbocharger and EGR valve clogging on high-mileage examples, particularly those used for short journeys without regular regeneration cycles. Engine code(s): DV4 Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 206, 307, Partner, Bipper Production years: 2001–2014 Common failure points: Turbocharger coking, EGR valve blockage, injector seal leaks
items.cta: Get quotes for Peugeot 1.4L diesel engines

items.title: Peugeot 1.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.5 BlueHDi (DV5) replaced the 1.6 HDi in many models from 2018 onward, offering lower CO₂ emissions but introducing significant reliability concerns. The most notorious issue affects the 1.5 BlueHDi’s 7mm timing chain, which can wear prematurely due to combustion residue and high pressure. Stellantis issued a global recall affecting nearly 240,000 vehicles, including the Peugeot 308, 3008, and 5008 from 2017–2023, replacing the chain with an upgraded 8mm unit and extending warranty coverage to 10 years or 240,000 km. Engine code(s): DV5RC, DV5R, YHZ, AH01 Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 308, 3008, 5008, Rifter, Partner (2018–2023) Production years: 2018–present Common failure points: 7mm timing chain failure (recall MXL), AdBlue crystallisation, EGR cooler blockage
items.cta: Get quotes for Peugeot 1.5L diesel engines

items.title: Peugeot 1.6L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.6 HDi (DV6) is the most widely used Peugeot diesel engine in the UK, fitted to hundreds of thousands of 208, 308, 3008, 508, Partner, and Expert models. The DV6TED4 variant is particularly common. While generally durable, these engines suffer from turbo oil feed pipe coking (leading to turbo failure and bottom-end damage), injector seal leaks, and clogged DPF/FAP filters on vehicles used predominantly for short trips. Engine code(s): DV6, DV6TED4, 9H06, 9HZ Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 208, 308, 3008, 508, Partner, Expert, Bipper Production years: 2005–2018 Common failure points: Turbo oil feed coking, injector seal failure, DPF clogging, EGR valve
items.cta: Get quotes for Peugeot 1.6L diesel engines

items.title: Peugeot 1.9L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 1.9L DW8 engine is the old-guard workhorse of Peugeot’s 306, 406, and Expert van ranges. These indirect-injection (IDI) units are famously robust, albeit noisy and underpowered by modern standards. The most common issue is injector leakage and fuel system air ingress due to ageing seals and perished return hoses — rarely a replacement trigger, but a persistent annoyance for owners of older Peugeots. Engine code(s): DW8 Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 306, 406, Expert, Boxer Production years: 1998–2006 Common failure points: Injector seal leaks, fuel system air ingress, glow plug failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Peugeot 1.9L diesel engines

items.title: Peugeot 2.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.0 HDi (DW10) powers a vast range of Peugeot models from the late 1990s through the 2010s, including the 306, 307, 308, 406, 407, 3008, 5008, Expert, and Boxer. The DW10 is generally considered one of Peugeot’s most robust diesel engines, but high-mileage units suffer from injector seal failure (diesel contamination of engine oil), turbo variable vane sticking, and clogged EGR valves. The earlier RHY (90bhp) and RHS (110bhp) variants are particularly simple, with injectors that do not require coding. Engine code(s): DW10, DW10BTED4, DW10CB, RHY, RHS Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 306, 307, 308, 406, 407, 3008, 5008, Expert, Boxer Production years: 1999–2015 Common failure points: Injector seal diesel-in-oil contamination, turbo vanes sticking, EGR valve
items.cta: Get quotes for Peugeot 2.0L diesel engines

items.title: Peugeot 2.2L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.2 HDi (DW12) was fitted to larger Peugeot models including the 607, 807, and Boxer van. It offered greater torque for towing and heavy-duty applications but introduced complexity with twin balancing shafts and a more sophisticated fuel system. Common issues include injector seal failure and DPF clogging, though catastrophic failures are less common than on smaller-displacement HDi units. Engine code(s): DW12 Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 607, 807, Boxer Production years: 2000–2010 Common failure points: Injector seal leaks, DPF clogging, EGR valve failure
items.cta: Get quotes for Peugeot 2.2L diesel engines

items.title: Peugeot 2.5L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.5L DK5 diesel is a rare engine, derived from the older XUD family and fitted primarily to Peugeot’s J5 and Boxer vans in the 1990s. These indirect-injection units were robust but loud and smoky by modern standards. Given their age, replacement engines are increasingly difficult to source. Engine code(s): DK5 Compatible models (UK): Peugeot J5, Boxer (1990s) Production years: 1990–1998 Common failure points: Head gasket failure, injection pump seal leaks, general age-related degradation
items.cta: Get quotes for Peugeot 2.5L diesel engines

items.title: Peugeot 2.7L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 2.7L V6 diesel (DT17) was a shared PSA-Ford-Jaguar unit fitted to the Peugeot 607 and 407 Coupe. It offered refined V6 performance but is complex and expensive to maintain. Common failures include turbocharger issues and EGR cooler blockage. Replacement engines are rare and costly. Engine code(s): DT17 Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 607, 407 Coupe Production years: 2004–2010 Common failure points: Turbo failure, EGR cooler blockage, injector seal leaks
items.cta: Get quotes for Peugeot 2.7L diesel engines

items.title: Peugeot 3.0L Diesel Engines
items.description: The 3.0L V6 diesel (DT20) replaced the 2.7L in later 607 and 407 Coupe models, also appearing in the 508 RXH. This engine offered 240bhp and refined performance but carried over many of the DT17’s complexity. Timing chain stretch and turbocharger issues are the primary replacement drivers due to the high cost of out-of-warranty repairs. Engine code(s): DT20 Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 607, 407 Coupe, 508 RXH Production years: 2009–2014 Common failure points: Timing chain stretch, turbocharger failure, EGR cooler blockage
items.cta: Get quotes for Peugeot 3.0L diesel engines

items.title: Peugeot 1.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.0L petrol engine appeared in Peugeot’s smallest city cars across multiple generations. The early TU9 powered the 106 and 205, while the later EB0 PureTech (999cc) found its way into the 108 and 208. The EB0 is a three-cylinder unit that shares the wet belt design of its larger 1.2 PureTech sibling, making belt degradation at approximately 50,000–60,000 miles the primary replacement driver. Engine code(s): TU9, EB0 (EB2 variants) Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 106, 205, 108, 208 (entry-level) Production years: 1991–2003 (TU9), 2014–2021 (EB0) Common failure points: EB0: wet belt degradation; TU9: carburettor issues, head gasket failure
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 1.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 1.1L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.1L TU1 engine was the entry-level petrol unit across the 106, 206, and 205 ranges. Simple, robust, and non-interference (valves do not strike pistons if timing belt fails), these engines rarely require full replacement. The TU1JP (code HFX/HFZ) was the final evolution, fitted to the 206 until the mid-2000s. Engine code(s): TU1, TU1JP, HFX, HFZ Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 106, 205, 206, 306 (base models) Production years: 1990–2006 Common failure points: Head gasket failure, distributor cap wear, carburettor issues (early)
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 1.1L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 1.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.2L family spans from the older XZ engine in the 104/205 to the modern EB2 PureTech three-cylinder. The PureTech (2012–present) is now Peugeot’s most controversial petrol engine. Its wet timing belt runs inside the engine oil and degrades prematurely, blocking the oil pickup strainer and causing catastrophic oil starvation. The EB2 is fitted to the 208, 2008, 308, 3008, and Rifter, with affected models from 2012–2024. Replacement costs range from £2,800–£4,000 for a rebuilt unit. Engine code(s): XZ, TU2, EB2, EB2ADTX, EB2DTS, HM01, HNZ Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 104, 205, 106, 208, 2008, 308, 3008, Rifter Production years: 1972–1988 (XZ), 2012–present (EB2) Common failure points: EB2: wet belt degradation leading to oil starvation (primary failure); XZ/TU2: carburettor issues
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 1.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 1.3L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.3L petrol engines belong to Peugeot’s early lineage. The TM engine powered the 203, while the TU2 (1,294cc) appeared in niche applications. These are rare engines in the UK replacement market due to their age. Most demand comes from classic car restorers rather than daily drivers. Engine code(s): TM, TU2 (1.3L variant) Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 203, 104, 205 (early) Production years: 1948–1960 (TM), 1980s (TU2) Common failure points: Age-related seal degradation, carburettor issues, parts scarcity
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 1.3L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 1.4L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.4L petrol spanned multiple families: the XY and TU3 in the 205, 106, 206, and 306, and the later EP3 (Prince engine) in the 207 and 308. The EP3, co-developed with BMW, introduced variable valve timing but also timing chain tensioner issues. The TU3 remains one of Peugeot’s most reliable small petrols, with head gasket failure being the most common major repair. Engine code(s): XY, TU3, EP3, KFV Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 205, 106, 206, 306, 207, 1007, 308 Production years: 1975–2014 Common failure points: Head gasket failure (TU3), timing chain tensioner (EP3), ignition coil failure
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 1.4L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 1.5L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.5L petrol is a rare displacement in Peugeot history, appearing as the TN3 in the 203 and as the TU4 in certain 106 and 205 variants, primarily for non-UK markets. These are not common replacement engines in the UK today. Engine code(s): TN3, TU4, XB2, XB5 Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 203, 106 (limited), 205 (limited) Production years: 1950s–1990s (limited) Common failure points: Age-related failure, parts scarcity
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 1.5L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 1.6L Petrol Engines – TU5 / EC5
items.description: The 1.6L TU5 engine is one of Peugeot’s most reliable and widely used petrol units, fitted to the 206, 306, 307, and 207. The later EC5 variant (2012–present) added direct injection and VVT but is primarily found in developing markets. The TU5’s main weakness is coil pack failure and oil leaks from the camshaft seals. These engines rarely require full replacement — typically only after severe overheating or timing belt failure. Engine code(s): TU5, EC5 Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 206, 306, 307, 207, 308 (EC5 limited) Production years: 1996–2014 Common failure points: Coil pack failure, camshaft seal oil leaks, head gasket failure (overheating)
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 1.6L Petrol Engines – Prince (EP6 / THP)
items.description: The 1.6 Prince engine (EP6) was co-developed with BMW for the 207, 208, 308, RCZ, and 3008. The turbocharged THP variants (EP6CDT / EP6DTS) produce 150–200bhp but suffer from timing chain tensioner failure (chain rattle on cold start), high-pressure fuel pump failure (metal debris in oil), and coolant pump pulley actuator failure on the 208. These are the most common 1.6 petrol replacement engines in the UK. Engine code(s): EP6, EP6C, EP6CDT, EP6DTS, 5G06, 5FV Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 207, 208, 308, RCZ, 3008 Production years: 2006–2019 Common failure points: Timing chain tensioner failure, high-pressure fuel pump failure, coolant pump actuator (208), valve stem seal wear
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 1.6L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 1.8L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 1.8L petrol powered the 306, 405, and 406 in the 1990s and early 2000s, using the EW7 engine. These units are generally robust but suffer from coil pack failure and oil leaks from the cam cover gasket. The later EC8 (1.8L) variant was primarily for non-UK markets. Engine code(s): EW7, EC8 (limited UK), XC series (older) Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 306, 405, 406 Production years: 1993–2004 Common failure points: Coil pack failure, oil leaks, distributor cap issues (early)
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 1.8L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 2.0L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.0L petrol family includes the ZEJ (Douvrin) engine in the 305 and 405, the EW10 in the 206 GTI, 307, 406, and 407, and the EW10J4 in performance applications. The XU10J2/RS (used in the 205 GTI 1.9) was also 2.0L in some markets. The EW10 is generally reliable but suffers from coil pack failure, oil burning, and variable valve timing solenoid issues on later variants. Engine code(s): ZEJ, EW10, EW10J4, XU10 Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 305, 405, 206 GTI, 307, 406, 407 Production years: 1977–2010 Common failure points: Coil pack failure, oil consumption, VVT solenoid failure (late EW10)
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 2.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 2.2L Petrol Engines
items.description: The 2.2L EW12 powered the 406 Coupe, 407, and 607 as a smoother, more refined alternative to the 2.0L. These engines are relatively rare in the UK. Common issues include coil pack failure and oil leaks from the cam cover and crankshaft seals. Engine code(s): EW12, ZDJ Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 406 Coupe, 407, 607 Production years: 1999–2010 Common failure points: Coil pack failure, oil seal leaks, timing belt tensioner
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 2.2L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 2.7L Petrol Engines (PRV V6)
items.description: The 2.7L PRV V6 (ZM engine) was fitted to the 604 and 505 in the 1970s and 1980s. This SOHC V6 was shared with Renault and Volvo. Given the age of these vehicles, replacement engines are rare and typically sourced from specialist classic car breakers. Engine code(s): ZM (PRV) Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 604, 505 Production years: 1975–1985 Common failure points: Age-related seal and gasket failure, carburettor or injection system issues, parts scarcity
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 2.7L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 2.9L / 3.0L Petrol Engines (PRV V6 & ES9)
items.description: The 3.0L displacement appeared in two forms: the PRV V6 (ZN/ZP) in the 604 and 505, and the later ES9 V6 fitted to the 406 Coupe, 607, and 807. The ES9 is a 24-valve DOHC unit (2,946cc) offering 207bhp. Timing belt changes are critical (every 60,000 miles) as the ES9 is an interference engine. Coil pack failure and oil leaks are the most common issues. Engine code(s): ES9, ZN, ZP Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 406 Coupe, 607, 807, 604, 505 Production years: 1974–1998 (PRV), 1999–2010 (ES9) Common failure points: Coil pack failure, oil leaks (ES9), timing belt failure (interference engine → catastrophic)
items.cta: Compare Peugeot 3.0L petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot 1.6L Hybrid Engines (Hybrid4 & Hybrid 225)
items.description: Peugeot’s Hybrid4 system paired a 2.0L BlueHDi diesel (DW10) with an electric motor in the 3008 and 508 RXH, while the newer Hybrid 225 combines a 1.6L PureTech petrol (EP6FADTX) with an 81kW electric motor in the 3008, 308, and 508. The diesel hybrid system is complex and costly to repair — electric motor failure or battery degradation often exceeds the vehicle’s value. The petrol hybrid is newer, with fewer reported failures, but high-voltage component replacement requires specialist training. System / motor code(s): EP6FADTX (petrol), DW10 (diesel hybrid), electric rear axle motor Compatible models (UK): Peugeot 3008 Hybrid4, 508 RXH, 3008 Hybrid 225, 308 Hybrid 225, 508 Hybrid Production years: 2012–2019 (diesel hybrid), 2019–present (petrol hybrid) Important notes: Diesel Hybrid4 systems are complex and expensive — many are written off after electric motor or battery failure. Petrol hybrid batteries carry 8-year/100,000-mile manufacturer warranty. Closing line: Not sure which engine size your Peugeot needs? items.title: Peugeot Diesel Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Diesel has powered the majority of UK Peugeot sales for two decades, from the robust 1.9 DW8 in the 306 to the high-volume 1.6 HDi (DV6) in the 308 and 3008, and the more recent but problematic 1.5 BlueHDi (DV5) fitted to models from 2018 onwards – diesel engine replacement enquiries still dominate our marketplace. Common Peugeot Diesel Engine Families: DV4 – 1.4L entry-level diesel (206, 307, Partner); robust but turbo coking common. DV6 – 1.6 HDi (208, 308, 3008, 508, Partner, Expert); turbo oil feed coking is the main failure. DV5 – 1.5 BlueHDi (2018+ 308, 3008, 5008); timing chain and AdBlue system failures widespread. DW10 – 2.0 HDi (306, 307, 308, 406, 407, 3008, 5008, Expert, Boxer); the most reliable high-mileage Peugeot diesel. DW12 – 2.2 HDi (607, 807, Boxer); complex but less common in replacement data. DK5 – 2.5L (1990s J5, Boxer); now rare, age-related failure. Found In: Peugeot 206, 306, 307, 308 (1998–2018) – DV4, DV6, DW10 Peugeot 208, 2008, 3008, 5008 (2012–present) – DV6, DV5 Peugeot 406, 407, 508 (1998–2014) – DW10, DV6, DT20 Peugeot Partner, Expert, Boxer vans (1996–present) – DV4, DV6, DW10, DW12, DK5 Peugeot 607, 807 (2000–2010) – DW12, DT17, DT20 Known For (Brand-wide): Strong motorway fuel economy, especially on DW10 2.0 HDi models (50–60 mpg real-world) Turbo oil feed coking on DV6 (1.6 HDi) – the #1 reason for premature replacement AdBlue crystallisation and NOx countdown lockout on DV5 (1.5 BlueHDi) from 2018 DPF/FAP clogging on short-journey diesel cars across all families 1.5 BlueHDi timing chain recall (MXL) affecting ~240,000 Stellantis vehicles – upgraded 8mm chain now fitted to rebuilt units Around 65% of Peugeot engine replacement enquiries on our marketplace are diesel, with DV6 leading volume Typical Diesel Models (UK): Peugeot 308 1.6 HDi Peugeot 3008 1.5 BlueHDi Peugeot 208 1.6 HDi Peugeot Partner 1.6 HDi van Peugeot Expert 2.0 HDi van Peugeot Boxer 2.2 HDi
items.cta: Get quotes for Peugeot Diesel replacement engines

items.title: Peugeot Petrol Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Peugeot’s petrol range spans from the simple, robust TU engines of the 1990s and 2000s to the modern but problematic EB2 PureTech three-cylinder, whose wet timing belt degrades inside the engine oil – alongside the more powerful Prince (EP6) turbo engines in the RCZ and 308 GTI. Common Peugeot Petrol Engine Families: TU / ET – 1.0L (TU9), 1.1L (TU1), 1.4L (TU3), 1.6L (TU5); fitted to 106, 205, 206, 306, 307; very robust, head gasket main failure. EB (PureTech) – 1.0L (EB0), 1.2L (EB2, EB2ADTX, EB2DTS); 208, 2008, 308, 3008, Rifter; wet belt degradation leading to oil starvation – the most common petrol replacement. Prince (EP) – 1.4L (EP3), 1.6L naturally aspirated and turbo (EP6, 5G06); 207, 208, 308, RCZ, 3008; timing chain tensioner and high-pressure fuel pump failures. EW – 1.8L (EW7), 2.0L (EW10), 2.2L (EW12); 306, 406, 407, 607; coil pack failure and oil leaks. XU / PRV / ES – 2.0L, 2.7L, 3.0L (ES9); classic 205 GTI, 405, 406 Coupe, 607; interference timing belts require meticulous maintenance. Found In: Peugeot 106, 205, 206, 306, 307 (1990–2008) – TU, EW, XU Peugeot 207, 208, 2008, 308, RCZ, 3008 (2006–2019) – Prince (EP6), EB2 PureTech Peugeot 108, 208, 2008, 308, 3008, Rifter (2014–present) – EB0/EB2 PureTech series Peugeot 406 Coupe, 607, 807 (1999–2010) – EW10, EW12, ES9 V6 Known For (Brand-wide): 1.2 PureTech wet belt degradation (40,000–65,000 miles) – the most searched Peugeot petrol problem in the UK Prince engine (EP6) timing chain rattle on cold start – tensioner failure across 2006–2014 models TU series: near-bulletproof reliability but head gasket failure on higher-mileage 1.4L and 1.6L units Coil pack failure across EW10 and EW12 engines – often misdiagnosed as compression loss ES9 V6 (3.0L) interference timing belt – catastrophic valve/piston contact if belt snaps EB2 PureTech accounts for over 80% of petrol replacement enquiries on our marketplace Typical Petrol Models (UK): Peugeot 208 1.2 PureTech Peugeot 2008 1.2 PureTech Peugeot 308 1.2 PureTech Peugeot 308 GTI 1.6 THP Peugeot RCZ 1.6 THP Peugeot 206 1.4 / 1.6
items.cta: Compare Peugeot Petrol engine prices

items.title: Peugeot Plug‑in Hybrid (PHEV) Engines
items.description: Short descriptor: Peugeot’s plug-in hybrid range combines a 1.6L PureTech petrol engine (EP6FADTX) with an 81kW electric motor and 12.4kWh battery, launched on the 3008 Hybrid4, 308 Hybrid, 508 Hybrid, and 2008 Hybrid from 2019 – replacement engines remain rare but are available through specialist channels. Common Peugeot PHEV Engine Families: EP6FADTX (PureTech PHEV) – 1.6L turbo petrol (180bhp) paired with electric motor (110bhp), total system output up to 225bhp / 360bhp (4xe variants) How Peugeot Plug‑in Hybrid Systems Work (brand-wide): Petrol engine drives front wheels; electric motor drives rear wheels (e‑AWD) or assists front Electric-only range of approximately 30–40 miles (WLTP) depending on model The combustion engine block is shared with non-hybrid EP6 – but hybrid-specific ancillaries differ Replacement petrol engines must be hybrid-coded (EP6FADTX) – standard EP6 units are not a direct swap Found In: Peugeot 3008 Hybrid4 / Hybrid 225 (P84, 2019–present) Peugeot 308 Hybrid 180 / 225 (P5, 2021–present) Peugeot 508 Hybrid 180 / 225 (R83, 2019–present) Peugeot 2008 Hybrid (e‑2008 shares platform but primarily BEV – see below) Important Notes (Brand-wide): The 1.6 PureTech PHEV engine block is similar to non-hybrid EP6, but ancillaries (starter, alternator, coolant circuits) are hybrid-specific High-voltage battery and electric motor failures are more common than combustion engine failure on PHEVs Hybrid engine replacement requires specialist EV-safe workshop certification – not all Peugeot dealers offer this Always request a hybrid system health report alongside any PHEV engine quote Rebuilt PHEV engines through our network carry the same 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty
items.cta: Enquire about Peugeot Plug‑in Hybrid engine availability

items.title: Peugeot Electric Powertrains (BEV)
items.description: Short descriptor: Peugeot’s fully electric range (e‑208, e‑2008, e‑308, e‑Rifter, e‑Traveller) uses the Stellantis e‑CMP / e‑CMP2 platform with a single electric motor (100kW–115kW) mounted at the front, driving the front wheels – electric motor failure is extremely rare, but HV battery module repair is more common. Common Peugeot Electric Motors: EMotor (100kW/115kW) – single front-mounted motor, 136bhp / 156bhp variants; used across all e‑208, e‑2008, e‑308, e‑Rifter, e‑Traveller (2020–present) How Peugeot Electric Powertrains Work: Single motor, battery-only propulsion – no gearbox in traditional sense; single-speed reduction gear Typical range: 200–250 miles WLTP depending on model and battery (50kWh or 54kWh usable) Motor failure is extremely rare – most "engine" enquiries relate to reduction gear noise (often misdiagnosed) or HV battery degradation Our network connects you with HV battery and motor repair specialists, not traditional engine rebuilders Found In: Peugeot e‑208 (2020–present) – 100kW/136bhp motor, 50kWh battery Peugeot e‑2008 (2020–present) – 100kW/136bhp motor, 50kWh battery Peugeot e‑308 (2023–present) – 115kW/156bhp motor, 54kWh battery Peugeot e‑Rifter (2021–present) – 100kW motor, 50kWh battery Peugeot e‑Traveller (2020–present) – 100kW motor, 50kWh or 75kWh battery Important Notes: No traditional "engine" to replace – electric motor repair or HV battery module replacement is the equivalent combustion‑era enquiry Labour rates for EV work differ from combustion engine specialists – ensure your supplier is EV-certified Reduction gear (single-speed transmission) noise is sometimes mistaken for motor failure – independent diagnosis recommended HV battery health (State of Health / SoH) is critical – always request a SoH report before purchasing a used electric Peugeot We connect you with UK Peugeot EV specialists who offer motor refurbishment and battery module repair

engineCodeDirectory.h2: Peugeot Engine Codes – Most Replaced Engines (2008–2025)
engineCodeDirectory.intro: Every major Peugeot engine code from the last two decades, grouped by family, with compatible models and typical reasons why owners seek replacement. Select your engine to compare rebuilt prices and get quotes from UK Peugeot specialists.

entries.title: EB2 1.2L PureTech Petrol Engine
entries.description: The EB2 (including EB2ADTX and EB2DTS variants) powers the 208, 2008, 308, 3008, 5008 and Rifter from 2012 onwards. Production years: 2012–present. The #1 failure driving replacement is wet belt degradation: the timing belt runs inside the engine oil, disintegrates into rubber fragments, and blocks the oil pickup strainer – leading to oil starvation and complete bottom-end seizure. A quality rebuilt unit includes a revised wet belt system, upgraded oil pump components, and a fully cleaned oil gallery and sump.
entries.cta: Get quotes for EB2 Peugeot engine replacement

entries.title: EB0 1.0L PureTech Petrol Engine
entries.description: Found in the 108 and early 208 models (2014–2020). This smaller-capacity PureTech shares the same wet belt architecture as the EB2. Failure mileage tends to be higher (50,000–80,000 miles), but once the belt degrades, oil starvation kills the camshaft journals and turbocharger bearings. Rebuilt units include upgraded belt materials and a redesigned oil pump drive.

entries.title: EP6DT / EP6DTS 1.6L Turbo Petrol (THP150 / THP175) Engine
entries.description: Fitted to the 207 GT, 207 GTi, 308, 3008, RCZ and 508 from 2007 to 2016. Power outputs range from 150 HP (EP6DT) to 175 HP (EP6DTS). The most common catastrophic failures are timing chain stretch (original tensioner design fails, allowing chain slap and guide breakage) and carbon build-up on direct-injection intake valves – leading to misfires, loss of power, and eventual valve damage. A quality rebuilt unit includes the upgraded V2 timing chain kit, fully decarbonised cylinder head, and a revised high-pressure fuel pump.
entries.cta: Get quotes for EP6 THP Peugeot engine replacement

entries.title: EP6CDT 1.6L Turbo Petrol (THP200) Engine
entries.description: Found exclusively in the RCZ (2009–2015) and 308 GTi (2015–2017) producing 200 HP. This high-output variant suffers from the same timing chain stretch as lower-power THP engines, but with additional risk of piston ring land failure under hard use. Rebuilt units include forged pistons (on performance rebuilds), upgraded timing chain kit, and a fully reconditioned cylinder head with revised valve guide seals.

entries.title: EP6C 1.6L Naturally Aspirated Petrol Engine
entries.description: Fitted to the 207, 308, 3008 and Partner (2006–2014) producing 120 HP. Without turbocharging, the primary failure is valve timing solenoid failure and carbon build-up on intake valves (direct injection). Rebuilt units include decarbonised heads and upgraded camshaft position sensors.
entries.cta: Find rebuilt EP6C Peugeot 1.6L petrol engines

entries.title: DV6C / DV6FD 1.6L BlueHDi Diesel Engine
entries.description: Powering the 208, 2008, 308, 3008, 508, 5008, Partner and Expert from 2013 to 2023 (75–120 HP). The most common failure driving replacement is turbocharger seizure (variable geometry vanes stick due to carbon build-up) and injector seal leaks allowing combustion gases into the fuel system. A quality rebuilt unit includes a fully refurbished or new turbocharger, new injector seals, and a professionally cleaned DPF and EGR system.
entries.cta: Get quotes for DV6 BlueHDi Peugeot engine replacement

entries.title: DV6CTED 1.6L BlueHDi 120 Diesel Engine
entries.description: A higher-output DV6 variant found in the 208, 308, 508, 3008 and 5008 (2014–2021) producing 120 HP. AdBlue system crystallisation (blocked injector and NOx sensors) triggers engine derate and "NO START" countdowns – a very common replacement driver in high-mileage fleet vehicles. Rebuilt units include a fully refurbished AdBlue system or professional software deletion (customer choice) plus a new injector.

entries.title: DV5RC / DV5RCE 1.5L BlueHDi 130 Diesel Engine
entries.description: Fitted to the 208, 2008, 308, 3008, 5008, Rifter, Partner and Traveller from 2018 onwards (130 HP). The primary failures are EGR valve blockage (from short-trip driving) and AdBlue crystallisation – both trigger engine derate and reduced performance. Rebuilt units include a professionally cleaned EGR system, refurbished AdBlue injector, and upgraded turbo vane mechanism.
entries.cta: Find rebuilt DV5 BlueHDi Peugeot engines

entries.title: DW10BTED4 2.0L HDi Diesel Engine
entries.description: One of Peugeot's most widely produced diesel engines, fitted to the 307, 308, 407, 508, 3008, 5008, 607, Expert and Boxer from 2005 to 2016 (136–163 HP). DPF clogging (from incomplete regenerations) and EGR valve failure are the most common replacement drivers. Injector seal leaks and turbo wastegate sticking also feature heavily. A quality rebuilt unit includes a professionally cleaned or new DPF, refurbished EGR valve, and new injector seals.
entries.cta: Get quotes for DW10 HDi Peugeot engine replacement

entries.title: DW10ATED4 2.0L HDi Diesel Engine
entries.description: The earlier 90–110 HP variant fitted to the 307, 406, 607, Expert and Boxer from 1999 to 2009. Turbocharger failure (wastegate mechanism seizes) and injector pump wear are the primary failure points. Rebuilt units include a fully reconditioned turbocharger, new injector pump, and timing belt kit.

entries.title: DW12BTED4 2.2L HDi Diesel Engine
entries.description: Found in the 407, 508, 607, 807, Boxer and Expert from 2006 to 2016 (156–204 HP). The most common failure driving replacement is injector failure (piezo injectors leak or stick) and turbocharger seizure on the high-output variants. Rebuilt units include new or refurbished piezo injectors, a fully reconditioned turbo, and a replaced timing belt kit.
entries.cta: Find rebuilt DW12 2.2L HDi Peugeot engines

entries.title: 4HU 2.2L Diesel Engine (Boxer)
entries.description: Fitted exclusively to the Peugeot Boxer van (2006–2019) producing 100–130 HP. This Ford-sourced Puma engine suffers from injector seal failure (combustion gas blow-by) and turbocharger sticking. High-mileage commercial units often need full replacement at 120,000–180,000 miles. Rebuilt units include new injector seals, a fully reconditioned turbo, and a replaced timing belt kit.
entries.cta: Get quotes for 4HU Peugeot Boxer engine replacement

entries.title: TU5JP4 1.6L Petrol Engine
entries.description: Powering the 206, 207, 306, 307, 308, 406, Partner and Expert from 2000 to 2012 (110 HP). Head gasket failure (coolant leaks into cylinders) and camshaft wear at high mileage are the most common replacement drivers. Rebuilt units include a multi-layer steel (MLS) upgraded head gasket, reground camshafts, and a full timing belt kit.
entries.cta: Compare TU5JP4 1.6L Peugeot petrol engine prices

entries.title: XU10J4RS 2.0L 16V Petrol Engine (GTi-6)
entries.description: The legendary performance engine from the 306 GTi-6, 405 Mi16 and 406 Coupe (1993–2003, 167 HP). High-mileage units suffer from valve guide wear and timing belt failure (interference engine – belt snap kills valves and pistons). Rebuilt units include new valve guides, upgraded timing belt tensioner, and fully balanced bottom end.
entries.cta: Find rebuilt XU10J4RS Peugeot GTi-6 engines

entries.title: XUD9 1.9L Diesel Engine
entries.description: Fitted to the 205, 306, 405, Partner and Expert from 1988 to 2002 (70–92 HP). This indirect injection unit is generally robust, but head gasket failure (coolant loss) and injector pump seal leaks drive most replacement demand. Rebuilt units include a new head gasket, refurbished injector pump, and fully serviced injectors. Closing line (Block A): Can’t find your engine code listed above? engineYears.h2: Peugeot Engines by Year – What Was Fitted & When

years.preview: TU Petrol and XUD Diesel: The Analogue Era 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The mid-1990s Peugeot UK range was built almost entirely around two legendary PSA engine families - the TU small-capacity petrol and the XUD indirect-injection diesel - both regarded by specialists as among the most mechanically robust engines ever fitted to mainstream European cars. Key Changes TU3 and TU5 petrol families cover 1.1, 1.4, and 1.6-litre capacity across all volume Peugeot models XUD 1.9 naturally aspirated and turbocharged diesel continues from late 1980s as the dominant Peugeot diesel unit Single-point and multi-point injection progressively replaces carburettor on TU petrol units from 1992 Euro 1 and Euro 2 compliance via revised catalyst and injection management - no EGR required at this stage 205, 306, and 106 are the primary UK-market models carrying both engine families Main Engines PSA TU3JP - 1.1-litre naturally aspirated petrol (Peugeot 106, Peugeot 205) PSA TU3 / TU3F - 1.4-litre naturally aspirated petrol (Peugeot 106, 306, 205) PSA TU5JP4 - 1.6-litre naturally aspirated petrol 16v, 88–100 bhp (Peugeot 306, 106 Rallye) PSA XUD7 - 1.8-litre naturally aspirated diesel (Peugeot 106, 205) PSA XUD9 - 1.9-litre naturally aspirated diesel, 69 bhp (Peugeot 306, 405) PSA XUD9TE - 1.9-litre turbocharged diesel, 90 bhp (Peugeot 306 TD, 405 TD) Popular Models Peugeot 106 (petrol and diesel) Peugeot 205 Peugeot 306 Peugeot 405 Known For XUD9 naturally aspirated diesel is widely regarded as virtually indestructible - documented examples exceeding 400,000 miles with basic maintenance and without major overhaul TU petrol family is extremely robust mechanically; primary wear items are the timing belt (service item every 72,000 miles) and water pump - inexpensive components with no surprise failure modes XUD turbocharged units can suffer injector pump governor spring fatigue at very high mileages - an inexpensive fix but diagnostic awareness is required Replacement demand for XUD and TU units remains active in the UK; a significant number of classic 106/205/306 owners are still maintaining these cars [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: TU3JP, TU3F, TU5JP4, XUD7, XUD9, XUD9TE
years.cta: Compare 1993–1999 Peugeot TU petrol and XUD diesel replacement engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: XU Petrol, HDi Arrives & the 206/406 Era 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Peugeot's 1999 introduction of the HDi common rail diesel in the 206 and 406 was a landmark moment for the brand - replacing the XUD with a new generation of high-pressure direct injection technology that transformed refinement and economy, while the XU-family petrols covered 1.6–2.0-litre duties across the mainstream range. Key Changes PSA HDi first-generation common rail diesel debuts in Peugeot 406 and 206 - initially 1.9 HDi (DW8) and 2.0 HDi (DW10) XU7 and XU10 petrol family covers 1.6, 1.8, and 2.0-litre naturally aspirated petrol across 306, 405, 406 Peugeot 206 launches 1998 as the brand's volume flagship - TU petrol and new DW8 HDi diesel at launch 206 becomes the UK's best-selling Peugeot ever; engine volumes are enormous, creating long-term parts demand Euro 3 compliance from 2000 - revised injection calibration and catalyst updates on XU petrol range 406 Coupe and 406 Estate with 2.0 and 2.2 HDi introduce more powerful DW10 variants to the UK fleet Main Engines PSA XU7JP4 - 1.8-litre naturally aspirated petrol 16v, 113 bhp (Peugeot 306, 406) PSA XU10J4R - 2.0-litre naturally aspirated petrol 16v, 136 bhp (Peugeot 406, 206 GTi) PSA DW8 (1.9 HDi) - 1.9-litre common rail diesel, 68–90 bhp (Peugeot 206, 306, 406) PSA DW10 (2.0 HDi) - 2.0-litre common rail diesel, 90–138 bhp (Peugeot 406, 607) PSA TU5JP4 - 1.6-litre naturally aspirated petrol 16v, 110 bhp (Peugeot 206, 206 GTi 110) Popular Models Peugeot 206 Peugeot 206 GTi 110 Peugeot 306 Peugeot 406 Peugeot 406 Coupe Known For DW8 1.9 HDi is a transitional unit - more refined than XUD but less reliable at high mileage; injector tip degradation and high-pressure pump wear are the two most common failure modes DW10 2.0 HDi in 8-valve guise (90–110 bhp) is one of the most durable Peugeot diesels in the modern range - smooth, long-lived, and widely supported by UK independent specialists XU10 2.0 16v petrol (as fitted to 206 GTi 180 and 306 GTi-6) is a high-revving, naturally aspirated unit with outstanding long-term mechanical reliability; no known structural weaknesses 206 1.4 TU petrol is very high-volume in the UK market - replacement engine demand remains consistently strong due to sheer fleet numbers still in circulation [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: XU7JP4, XU10J4R, DW8, DW10, TU5JP4, 9HX, 9HZ
years.cta: Find 1996–2004 Peugeot 206 and 406 HDi replacement engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: DV6/DV4 1.6 HDi, 307/407 Era & Euro 4 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The DV6 1.6 HDi launched in 2003 became the highest-volume Peugeot diesel engine in UK history - fitted across the 206, 207, 307, 308, 407, 508, Partner, Expert, and numerous Citroën platforms - but also became associated with a well-documented oil pump failure mode that remains the single most searched Peugeot engine fault on UK comparison sites. Key Changes DV6 1.6 HDi (9HX / 9HY / 9HZ codes) introduced 2003 - PSA's volume diesel replacing 1.9 XUD and DW8 across small and medium models DV4 1.4 HDi introduced for small car segment - Peugeot 206, 307, 1007 307 HDi is the UK's dominant medium family car diesel; 407 1.6 HDi and 2.0 HDi cover the executive segment Euro 4 compliance from 2005 across all HDi units via revised EGR calibration DW10C 2.0 HDi revised to 136 bhp in 407 and 607 - improved injection timing, variable geometry turbo standard 207 launches 2006 with DV6 1.6 HDi as the primary diesel option Main Engines PSA DV6 (9HX / 9HY / 9HZ) - 1.6-litre common rail diesel 8v, 90–110 bhp (Peugeot 206, 207, 307, 407, Partner) PSA DV4 (8HX / 8HZ) - 1.4-litre common rail diesel, 68–75 bhp (Peugeot 206, 307, 1007) PSA DW10C - 2.0-litre common rail diesel, 110–138 bhp (Peugeot 307 SW, 407, 607) PSA EW10 - 2.0-litre naturally aspirated petrol 16v (Peugeot 307, 407 petrol) PSA EW7 - 1.8-litre naturally aspirated petrol 16v (Peugeot 307) Popular Models Peugeot 307 HDi Peugeot 407 1.6 HDi Peugeot 407 2.0 HDi Peugeot 206 1.6 HDi Peugeot Partner 1.6 HDi Known For DV6 1.6 HDi oil pump failure is the single most reported Peugeot engine fault in UK motoring - the low-pressure oil pump located in the sump wears prematurely on neglected-service examples, causing catastrophic engine seizure with little warning Oil change intervals are critical on DV6: maximum 9,000 miles on 5W-30 C2 spec oil - extended intervals accelerate oil pump wear and turbo oil feed line clogging DW10 2.0 HDi 8v remains the most durable engine in this generation - the 16v high-power variants are more problematic with swirl flap failures adding to owner concern EGR valve carbon fouling on DV6 units is a maintenance-critical item - blocked EGR causes rough idle, limp mode, and accelerated injector wear if left unaddressed [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: 9HX, 9HY, 9HZ, DV6, 8HX, 8HZ, DV4, DW10C, EW10, EW7
years.cta: Compare 2002–2007 Peugeot 1.6 HDi DV6 and 2.0 HDi replacement engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: THP 1.6 Turbo Petrol, DV6 Euro 5 & 207/308/508 Generation 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The 2006 launch of the 1.6 THP turbocharged petrol - co-developed with BMW as the Prince engine family - introduced Peugeot's first mainstream turbocharged petrol to the volume range, offering genuine performance in the 207 and 308, but simultaneously inheriting the N14-generation timing chain concerns that would define the engine's reputation on the used market. Key Changes EP6 1.6 THP (Prince engine, co-developed with BMW) debuts in Peugeot 207 and 308 - 120–175 bhp depending on state of tune DV6 1.6 HDi updated to Euro 5 DPF specification from 2010 - new codes 9HC, 9HD, 9HF, 9HK DW10C 2.0 HDi Euro 5 introduced in new 508 and 3008/5008 - revised injection and EGR management 308 launches 2007 as 307 replacement; 207 replaces 206; both anchor the DV6 diesel and THP petrol range for this period Peugeot 508 launches 2011 replacing 407 - DW10 2.0 BlueHDi development begins RCZ sports coupe launches 2010 with THP 200/270 bhp variants - highest performance Peugeot petrol of the modern era Main Engines PSA/BMW EP6 (Prince) 1.6 THP - 1.6-litre turbocharged petrol, 120–175 bhp (Peugeot 207 GTi, 308, RCZ, 3008) PSA/BMW EP6 THP 200 - 1.6-litre turbocharged petrol, 200–270 bhp (Peugeot RCZ THP 200/270) PSA DV6 Euro 5 (9HC / 9HD / 9HF) - 1.6-litre common rail diesel DPF, 90–112 bhp (207, 308, 3008, 5008, Partner) PSA DW10C Euro 5 - 2.0-litre common rail diesel, 120–163 bhp (508, 407, 3008 HDi) PSA EW10 petrol - 2.0-litre naturally aspirated petrol (308 petrol continued) Popular Models Peugeot 207 GTi 1.6 THP Peugeot 308 1.6 THP Peugeot RCZ THP Peugeot 308 1.6 HDi Peugeot 508 2.0 HDi Peugeot 3008 1.6 HDi Known For EP6 1.6 THP timing chain tensioner wear is a documented and significant fault - identical in architecture to BMW's N14 issue; cold-start rattle is the primary symptom and early intervention is critical to avoid engine replacement EP6 cam phaser (variable valve timing actuator) failure generates a distinctive cold-start rattle and MIL illumination - a common high-mileage fault on 207 GTi and early 308 THP cars DV6 Euro 5 with DPF requires active regeneration cycle management - short-cycle urban driving prevents regeneration and causes blocked DPF faults from 80,000 miles onward RCZ THP 270 bhp variant is a highly stressed engine; head gasket stress and chain tensioner wear are accelerated at the higher boost levels of the JCW/THP 270 specification [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: EP6, EP6DT, EP6CDT, 9HC, 9HD, 9HF, DV6 Euro 5, DW10C Euro 5, EW10

years.preview: BlueHDi Euro 6, PureTech Launch & 208/2008/308 II Generation 🔴 Major Engine Family Introduction 🔴 Technology Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The 2012–2018 period was the most consequential engine transition in Peugeot's modern history - the BlueHDi common rail diesel achieved Euro 6 via SCR/AdBlue, the second-generation DW10 matured into its most reliable specification, and the 1.2 PureTech three-cylinder turbocharged petrol launched as the brand's entry into the downsizing era - introducing an engine that would become as well-known for its wet belt controversy as for its genuine everyday efficiency. Key Changes PureTech 1.2 (EB2 family) launches 2012 - naturally aspirated 82 bhp for 208, turbocharged 110/130 bhp from 2013 PureTech uses a wet timing belt (belt-in-oil) system - a critical design detail that becomes the brand's most controversial reliability concern BlueHDi 1.6 Euro 6 (DV6FC / 9BH) introduces SCR/AdBlue system - replaces EGR-only approach of earlier DV6 BlueHDi 2.0 Euro 6 (DW10FC) enters 508, 3008, 5008 range with AdBlue and revised injection New 208 (2012), 2008 (2013), and 308 II (2013) all launch with PureTech petrol and BlueHDi diesel as the primary powertrain offering Stop-start standard across all new Euro 6 Peugeot models from 2015 Main Engines PSA EB0 - 1.0-litre naturally aspirated petrol, 68 bhp (Peugeot 108, early 208) PSA EB2F - 1.2-litre naturally aspirated PureTech petrol, 82 bhp (Peugeot 208, 2008) PSA EB2DT / EB2ADT - 1.2-litre turbocharged PureTech petrol, 110 bhp (Peugeot 208, 2008, 308) PSA EB2DTS - 1.2-litre turbocharged PureTech petrol, 130 bhp (Peugeot 308, 3008, 5008) PSA DV6FC (9BH / BlueHDi 100/120) - 1.6-litre BlueHDi Euro 6 diesel (Peugeot 208, 308, 2008, 3008, Partner) PSA DW10FC - 2.0-litre BlueHDi Euro 6 diesel, 150 bhp (Peugeot 508, 3008, 5008) Popular Models Peugeot 208 (1.2 PureTech, 1.6 BlueHDi) Peugeot 2008 (1.2 PureTech, 1.6 BlueHDi) Peugeot 308 II (1.2 PureTech 130, 2.0 BlueHDi) Peugeot 3008 (1.2 PureTech, 2.0 BlueHDi) Peugeot 508 (2.0 BlueHDi) Peugeot Partner (1.6 BlueHDi) Known For PureTech 1.2 wet belt failure is the defining Peugeot engine story of this decade - belt degradation in oil causes rubber particles to circulate through the engine oil, clogging the oil pump filter and causing seizure with catastrophic and sudden consequences PSA issued a recall for the PureTech wet belt in 2021; Stellantis subsequently developed a timing chain conversion kit; early 2013–2017 PureTech cars should have documented wet belt service history or ideally the chain conversion fitted before purchase PureTech also suffers significant piston ring oil consumption on high-mileage examples - up to 1 litre per 1,000 miles has been reported; PCV valve replacement may help but ring replacement is the definitive fix BlueHDi DW10FC 2.0 is the most reliable and durable modern Peugeot diesel - well-documented to reach 250,000+ miles with correct 10,000-mile oil change intervals and functioning AdBlue system [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: EB0, EB2F, EB2DT, EB2ADT, EB2DTS, DV6FC, 9BH, DW10FC, BlueHDi 100, BlueHDi 120, BlueHDi 150
years.cta: Compare 2012–2018 Peugeot PureTech and BlueHDi replacement engine prices from UK specialists

years.preview: PureTech Revised, Euro 6d, 508/3008 II & PHEV Introduction 🔴 Major Reliability Revision 🔴 Technology Introduction
years.description: Short intro: The 2018–2023 period saw Peugeot's most important reliability revision - progressive PureTech engineering updates aimed at addressing wet belt degradation, the full Euro 6d rollout with updated BlueHDi SCR calibration, and the brand's first plug-in hybrid (PHEV) powertrains combining PureTech petrol with electric motors in the 3008, 508, and 508 SW. Key Changes PureTech revised: 2019–2021 production improvements include updated belt material, revised oil specification (Quartz 5W-30 mandatory), and revised PCV routing to reduce fuel dilution of oil PSA issues formal recall and extended warranty on PureTech wet belt across multiple models - 2021 action applies to pre-revision cars Peugeot 3008 PHEV (e-EAT8) and 508 PHEV launch 2019 - PureTech 180 petrol + 80 kW electric motor, 225 bhp combined, 35 miles EV range Euro 6d compliance across all new Peugeot models from 2020 - AdBlue AdBlue quantities increased, SCR catalyst efficiency improved New Peugeot 208 II and 2008 II (2019) introduce updated PureTech 75/100/130 bhp alongside e-208 and e-2008 BEV variants on the same platform BlueHDi 130 (1.5-litre DV5R) introduced replacing the DV6 1.6 in new 208 II, 2008 II, 308 II - smaller capacity, Euro 6d from launch, chain-driven Main Engines PSA EB2ADTD - 1.2-litre turbocharged PureTech 100 bhp Euro 6d (208 II, 2008 II, 308 III, Rifter) PSA EB2DTS - 1.2-litre turbocharged PureTech 130 bhp Euro 6d (208 II, 308, 3008, 5008) PSA EB2ADTX - 1.2-litre turbocharged PureTech 155 bhp Euro 6d (2008 GT, 308 III GT, 3008 GT) PSA DV5R / BlueHDi 130 - 1.5-litre common rail diesel, 130 bhp, chain-driven (208 II, 2008, 308 III, 3008) PSA DW10FC BlueHDi 180 - 2.0-litre diesel, 180 bhp (508, 3008, 5008 top spec) PureTech 180 + 80 kW e-Motor - PHEV combined 225 bhp (3008 Hybrid4, 508 PHEV, 508 SW PHEV) Popular Models Peugeot 208 II (PureTech and e-208) Peugeot 2008 II (PureTech and e-2008) Peugeot 308 III (PureTech 130/155, BlueHDi 130) Peugeot 3008 PHEV Peugeot 508 PHEV SW Known For Post-2021 PureTech builds are meaningfully improved but not entirely resolved - the chain conversion retrofit remains the most definitive fix for pre-revision cars; post-2021 production with correct service history is a safer purchase DV5R 1.5 BlueHDi 130 is a positive development - chain-driven, Euro 6d compliant from launch, and early reliability data is notably better than the DV6 units it replaces 3008 and 508 PHEV systems are capable but demand disciplined battery charging habits - owners who never plug in will see no fuel economy benefit and accelerate hybrid battery wear PureTech 155 bhp in 2008 GT specification is the highest-output PureTech 3-cylinder in volume production Peugeots - turbo reliability at higher boost levels is still being established across the owner community [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: EB2ADTD, EB2DTS, EB2ADTX, DV5R, DW10FC, PureTech 100, PureTech 130, PureTech 155, BlueHDi 130, PHEV eMotor 80kW
years.cta: Get 2018–2023 Peugeot PureTech and BlueHDi 130 replacement engine quotes from UK specialists

years.preview: e-208, e-2008, e-308 & the BEV Expansion 🔴 Technology Introduction
years.description: Short intro: Peugeot's electric offensive launched in 2019 with the e-208 and e-2008, built on Stellantis's CMP platform alongside their combustion counterparts - making the brand one of the first mainstream manufacturers to offer full BEV versions of their two highest-volume models simultaneously. Key Changes e-208 BEV launches late 2019 with 100 kW / 136 bhp eMotor, 50 kWh battery, 217 miles WLTP e-2008 BEV launches 2020 with same 100 kW eMotor and 50 kWh battery as e-208 2022 update increases e-208 power to 115 kW / 156 bhp and battery to 51 kWh - range increases to 248 miles WLTP e-308 and e-308 SW launch 2023 - 115 kW / 156 bhp, 54 kWh battery, up to 248 miles WLTP Peugeot e-Expert and e-Partner electric LCVs enter UK fleet market 2021 - same CMP/EMP2 platform eMotor suite All BEV models CMP platform - shared eMotor architecture allows greater parts commonality across the electric range Main Engines Stellantis CMP eMotor 100 kW - 136 bhp, 260 Nm, 50 kWh battery (Peugeot e-208 2019–2022, e-2008 2020–2022) Stellantis CMP eMotor 115 kW - 156 bhp, 260 Nm, 51–54 kWh battery (e-208 2023+, e-2008 2023+, e-308) Stellantis EMP2 eMotor 100 kW - 136 bhp (Peugeot e-Expert, e-Partner LCV) Popular Models Peugeot e-208 (2019–) Peugeot e-2008 (2020–) Peugeot e-308 (2023–) Peugeot e-308 SW (2023–) Peugeot e-Expert Peugeot e-Partner Known For e-208 is the UK's most popular small electric car - strong real-world urban range, fast 100 kW DC charging to 80% in under 30 minutes, and backed by the widest Peugeot dealer network of any BEV 2022 eMotor upgrade from 100 kW to 115 kW is a meaningful step - the revised powertrain is more efficient at motorway speeds and reduces energy consumption to approximately 12 kWh/100 km Battery pack and eMotor replacement currently requires Peugeot-authorised Stellantis tooling - independent EV specialists are beginning to offer battery health and cell replacement services on first-generation CMP units from 2023 onward e-208 battery pack degradation monitoring is recommended from 40,000 miles - first-generation 50 kWh packs showing 10–15% degradation on high-cycle examples is within manufacturer tolerance but should be tracked [Tags] Engine Codes Covered: CMP eMotor 100kW, CMP eMotor 115kW, EMP2 eMotor 100kW, e-208 BEV, e-2008 BEV, e-308 BEV

faq.h2: Peugeot Engine Replacement – Frequently Asked Questions
faq.intro: Quick, honest answers to the most common questions Peugeot owners ask before comparing engine replacement prices and finding a UK specialist.

items.question: How much does a Peugeot engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A rebuilt Peugeot engine typically costs between £2,200 and £4,500 depending on the engine family. A common 1.6 HDi (DV6) for a 308 or 3008 ranges from £2,400–£3,200 supply-only, while a 1.2 PureTech (EB2) for a 208 or 2008 is £2,600–£3,800. A 2.0 HDi (DW10) for a Boxer or Expert van runs £2,200–£3,400. Fitting adds £500–£900 at an independent specialist. Main dealers often charge 50–80% more for the same rebuilt unit. Compare Peugeot engine replacement prices now.
items.cta: Compare Peugeot engine prices now

items.question: What affects the cost of a Peugeot engine replacement?
items.answer: Four key factors determine your final cost: engine size and family (DV6/DV10 costs less than rare V6 DT20), condition (rebuilt with upgraded components costs more than used, but offers warranty), vehicle age and complexity (Boxer vans take longer than 208 hatchbacks), and supply-only versus supply-and-fit (add £500–£900). Location matters too — London and South East specialist rates are 20–30% higher than the North East or Scotland. Our tool shows you real quotes from across the UK, so you can compare transparently. Get quotes from UK Peugeot engine specialists.
items.cta: Compare Peugeot engine prices now

items.question: Is it worth replacing the engine in my Peugeot?
items.answer: In most cases, yes — especially if the chassis, gearbox, and body are in good condition. A rebuilt Peugeot engine with upgraded components (improved wet belt materials on EB2, revised chain tensioner on EP6, larger 8mm chain on DV5) often outlasts the original engine. Compare that to buying a replacement vehicle: a Peugeot 3008 with a failed 1.5 BlueHDi might be worth £4,000–£7,000 repaired versus £2,000–£3,000 as a non-runner. If your Peugeot holds value and has no major body corrosion, engine replacement is almost always worth it. Use our free comparison tool to see whether a rebuilt or used Peugeot engine makes financial sense.
items.cta: Get a Peugeot engine quote now

items.question: What's included in a Peugeot engine supply and fit service?
items.answer: A full supply-and-fit service includes sourcing a rebuilt or reconditioned unit matched to your exact engine code, removal of the failed engine, full installation of the replacement, new oil, filters, coolant, and timing belt/chain where required, plus a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty. The specialist also disposes of your old engine legally and test-drives the vehicle to confirm correct operation. Typical fitting cost is £500–£900, though some specialists bundle it into a single all-in price. Always ask whether the quote includes VAT, delivery, and any ancillary parts (like engine mounts or gaskets). Compare Peugeot engine supply and fit deals.
items.cta: Get a Peugeot engine quote now

items.question: How long does a Peugeot engine replacement take?
items.answer: For common Peugeot models like a 308 or 208 with a 1.6 HDi or 1.2 PureTech, a competent specialist will complete the swap in 3–5 working days. More complex models like a 3008 Hybrid4 or Boxer van with engine bay access challenges take 5–7 days. The biggest variable is engine availability — our network regularly stocks high-demand codes like DV6, DV5, EB2, and DW10, which keeps lead times to a minimum. For rare engines like the DT20 V6 or older XUD units, sourcing may add 5–10 days. Ask your chosen supplier for a confirmed lead time before booking. Get quotes from UK Peugeot engine specialists with confirmed lead times.
items.cta: Get a Peugeot engine quote now

items.question: Do reconditioned Peugeot engines come with warranty?
items.answer: Yes — every engine supplied through EngineMarket carries a minimum 12‑month unlimited mileage warranty covering all major internal components: crankshaft, conrods, pistons, timing chain or belt, oil pump, bearings, gaskets, and seals. Many specialists offer extended 24‑month or 50,000‑mile coverage on premium rebuilt units, particularly for popular families like DV6 and EB2 where upgraded components are used. Warranty excludes ancillaries (turbo, injectors, fuel pump) unless explicitly listed, and damage from external issues like overheating after fitting. Always confirm exact terms — including labour coverage — directly with your supplier before purchase. Find reconditioned Peugeot engines with comprehensive warranty cover.
items.cta: Find Peugeot engines with warranty

items.question: Should I choose a used or reconditioned Peugeot engine?
items.answer: A used Peugeot engine is cheaper upfront — typically £600–£1,200 for a DV6 or EB2 — but carries two significant risks: unknown history (was it a wet belt failure donor?) and no warranty beyond 30–90 days. A reconditioned or rebuilt engine costs £2,200–£4,000 but includes new piston rings, bearings, seals, timing components, a full clean, and a 12‑month warranty. For failure‑prone engines like the 1.2 PureTech (wet belt) or 1.5 BlueHDi (original 7mm chain), a reconditioned unit with upgraded parts is strongly recommended. Used is only sensible as a short-term fix or if you plan to sell the car immediately. Compare used vs reconditioned Peugeot engine prices now.

items.answer: EngineMarket connects you with a UK-wide network of independent Peugeot engine specialists, ranging from dedicated French car rebuilders in the Midlands (e.g., Leicester, Coventry) to national van specialists covering Partner and Expert fleets. Simply enter your registration number, and our tool matches you with nearby suppliers who stock your specific engine code and offer fitted supply. If you prefer supply-only, we also connect you with rebuilders who ship nationwide — delivery typically £60–£120. No need for main dealer labour rates. Compare Peugeot engine specialists near you.

items.question: What are the most common Peugeot engine failures?
items.answer: The five most common Peugeot engine failures driving replacement demand are: (1) 1.2 PureTech (EB2) wet belt degradation — belt debris blocks oil pickup, causing complete oil starvation from 40,000 miles; (2) 1.5 BlueHDi (DV5) 7mm timing chain failure — chain snaps or jumps timing (recall MXL, 2017–2023); (3) 1.6 HDi (DV6) turbo oil feed coking — kills turbo then bottom-end bearings; (4) 1.6 THP (EP6) timing chain tensioner failure — chain rattle then jump, plus high-pressure fuel pump debris; (5) 1.5 BlueHDi AdBlue crystallisation — blocks injector and lines, triggering NOx countdown lockout. All five failures typically make replacement the most economical outcome. Compare replacement engine prices for all Peugeot engine families.

items.question: How can I get the best price on a Peugeot engine?
items.answer: Always compare at least three quotes — prices for the same DV6 or EB2 engine code can vary by £1,000+ between specialists. Consider used (£600–£1,200) for very tight budgets or short-term ownership, but accept the risk. For long-term ownership, a reconditioned unit with upgraded components (£2,200–£4,000) is better value. Ask suppliers for a supply-only price if you have a trusted local garage — this removes the specialist's fitting markup. Always check whether delivery, fitting, and VAT are included in any headline price. Use our free comparison tool to get the best price on your Peugeot engine.
